docs.title: Welcome to RUNE
docs.text:
RUNE (Remediation & Unified Node Executor) is a focused, standards-driven automation framework for rapid, reliable remote remediation across Linux systems.

When an alert fires and time matters, RUNE lets operators and systems trigger structured, safe, repeatable actions — such as gathering logs, restarting services, or running diagnosis plugins — with a single CLI command or dashboard button.

RUNE is built for incident response, SoC operations, and SRE on-call workflows, not as yet another configuration-management clone.

docs.title: Why RUNE?
docs.text:
Most automation tools (Ansible, Salt, StackStorm, etc.) solve large, complex, stateful problems. RUNE solves a different one:

“Do this one action on that one node right now and give me structured output I can trust.”

RUNE is:

  • Agentless — remote hosts need nothing installed
  • Protocol-driven — RCS, EPS, and BPCS define all communication
  • Script-friendly — plugins are just Bash or Python
  • Machine-consumable — always returns structured JSON
  • Dashboards-ready — integrates cleanly with SoC and SRE tooling

RUNE is intentionally small — the scalpel that complements the automation sledgehammers.

Learn more: ➡️ Why RUNE?

docs.title: RUNE Glossary of Terms
docs.text: A reference for acronyms, modules, and naming conventions used throughout the RUNE framework.

docs.title: RUNE
docs.text: Remediation & Unified Node Executor — The name of the framework. Modular, CLI-driven orchestration engine for Linux environments.

docs.title: LOM
docs.text: Lifecycle Orchestration Module — Handles incoming execution requests, validates inputs, selects the correct plugin, and delegates to the LMM.

docs.title: LMM
docs.text: Local Mediation Module — Executes plugins remotely over SSH or SSM, captures output, enforces structured communication, and returns results.

docs.title: RCS
docs.text: Runtime Communication Specification — The protocol that defines how action execution messages are structured and routed.

docs.title: EPS
docs.text: Error Protocol Specification — Defines how errors are captured, formatted, and routed inside the RUNE system.

docs.title: MRS
docs.text: Module Registration Specification — Used to declare plugin/module capabilities (name, schema, version) for introspection and validation.

docs.title: Plugin
docs.text: A standalone Bash or Python script that conforms to the RUNE protocol and performs a specific system-level operation.

docs.title: BPCS
docs.text: Bash Plugin Communication Specification — Contract that all Bash-based plugins must follow. Input via stdin, output via stdout in JSON.

docs.title: CLI
docs.text: Command Line Interface — The rune terminal command used to run actions like rune run restart-docker --node web01

docs.title: SSM
docs.text: AWS Systems Manager — Optional agent-based transport method for executing remote commands without SSH.

docs.title: Trace ID / Span ID
docs.text: Unique identifiers used to track actions across distributed components and log trails.

docs.title: Job ID
docs.text: A unique identifier representing one execution instance of a plugin.

docs.title: Action
docs.text: The logical name of an operation that RUNE can execute, such as gather-logs, restart-docker, or noop.

docs.title: Plugin Directory
docs.text: Location where registered plugins reside. Example: plugins/ folder under project root.

docs.title: Payload
docs.text: The body of a structured JSON message that includes the data to be passed into or out of an action execution.

docs.title: exit code
docs.text:
Integer returned by a plugin indicating success (0) or failure (1+). Must be consistent with structured output.

This glossary will evolve as RUNE expands. PRs welcome for new terms.

docs.title: RUNE Development Roadmap
docs.text: This document outlines the staged development plan for RUNE, with the goal of balancing rapid MVP delivery against long-term architectural flexibility. Each phase builds on the last, expanding RUNE's capabilities without introducing bloat or unnecessary complexity.

docs.title: 🚀 Phase 1: MVP Delivery (Current Focus)
docs.text: Core goal: Execute healing and diagnostic operations on Linux nodes via CLI and/or dashboard API trigger, using remote plugin invocation over SSH or SSM.

docs.title: 🔧 Deliverables
docs.text:
• [x] rune_cli.py: Command-line interface with run subcommand
  • [x] orchestrator.py: Action validation, plugin resolution
  • [x] mediator.py: Remote plugin runner, I/O router
  • [x] transport_ssh.py: Execute plugin via SSH
  • [ ] transport_ssm.py: Execute plugin via AWS SSM (optional fallback)
  • [x] Bash plugins:
  • gather-logs.sh
  • restart-docker.sh
  • restart-nomad.sh
  • noop.sh
  • [x] Bash Plugin Communication Spec (BPCS v0.1)
  • [x] Structured stdout/stderr/output with JSON validation
  • [x] CLI flags: --dry-run, --output json|pretty, --use-ssm
  • [x] Logging & output capture to local logs or stdout

docs.title: 🧱 Phase 2: Framework Hardening
docs.text: Add developer-quality tooling, formal interfaces, and observability layers to stabilize RUNE as a platform.

docs.title: 🧪 Testing & Dev Tooling
docs.text:
• [ ] Unit test coverage for LOM and LMM
  • [ ] Integration test harness (mock transport layer)
  • [ ] Plugin test framework (stdin → plugin → JSON validator)
  • [ ] Basic telemetry for plugin runtime, failure rate, duration

docs.title: ⚙️ Plugin Runtime Improvements
docs.text:
• [ ] Versioned plugin discovery
  • [ ] Registry of known plugins with metadata (name, description, version, tags)
  • [ ] Environment validation prior to plugin exec (e.g., systemd available?)

docs.title: 🔐 Security / Access Control
docs.text:
• [ ] Execution audit trail (host, user, action, timestamp)
  • [ ] Optional sudo enforcement or policy filtering
  • [ ] CLI session token or execution fingerprint

docs.title: 🌐 Phase 3: Remote API + GUI Integration
docs.text: Enable RUNE to be triggered from external dashboards, alerting systems, or automation platforms.

docs.title: 📡 API & Dashboard Integration
docs.text:
• [ ] REST API to trigger actions (POST /run/<action>)
  • [ ] AuthN/AuthZ layer (token-based or OIDC)
  • [ ] Integration with SoC GUI to trigger RUNE via button
  • [ ] Response output routing to dashboard (WebSocket or polling)

docs.title: 🧠 Phase 4: Workflow & Orchestration Expansion
docs.text: Allow RUNE to execute multi-step workflows, aggregate outputs, and implement conditional or sequential logic.

docs.title: 🔄 Workflow Engine (Lightweight)
docs.text:
• [ ] Sequential action chaining
  • [ ] Conditional branching (if restart failed, gather-logs)
  • [ ] Parallel execution (across nodes)
  • [ ] Reusable workflow definitions in YAML/JSON

docs.title: 🔮 Phase 5: Enterprise Features & Ecosystem
docs.text: Solidify RUNE as a powerful, extensible automation fabric for large-scale Linux infrastructure.

docs.title: 🔐 Security & Policy
docs.text:
• [ ] Role-based policy to restrict plugins/actions per operator
  • [ ] Secrets integration (Vault, AWS SSM Parameter Store)

docs.title: 🌍 Ecosystem & Distribution
docs.text:
• [ ] Plugin packaging and versioning
  • [ ] CLI introspection: rune list-actions, rune plugin-info
  • [ ] Community plugin registry or sharing system

docs.title: 📊 Observability & Reporting
docs.text:
• [ ] Send trace data to ELK/Splunk
  • [ ] Correlation IDs and distributed trace support
  • [ ] Execution metrics dashboard (Grafana/Prometheus-friendly)

docs.title: 🧭 Roadmap Philosophy
docs.text:
• Build fast. Stay lean.
  • Prioritize simplicity, modularity, and explicit behavior.
  • Agentless first, agents only if necessary.
  • Always return structured output—RUNE should be machine- and human-readable.

This roadmap is a living document. Pull requests welcome for additions, edits, or discussion.

docs.title: Why RUNE?
docs.text:
RUNE exists to solve a gap that mainstream automation and orchestration platforms consistently overlook: fast, reliable, structured remote remediation without agents, daemons, or heavyweight infrastructure.

This document explains what makes RUNE unique, why it complements existing tools instead of competing with them, and how its standards-based design avoids becoming yet another proprietary "one‑off" automation system.

docs.title: 1. Purpose-Built for Remediation, Not Configuration
docs.text:
Most tools in this space were designed for one of the following:

  • Configuration management (Ansible, Puppet, Chef)
  • State-driven orchestration (Salt)
  • Workflow engines (StackStorm)
  • Monitoring and alerting (Sensu, Nagios)

RUNE is built for something different:

When a system is failing right now, a human needs a safe, repeatable way to run a specific remote action and get structured results immediately.

RUNE is laser-focused on:

  • Diagnostics
  • Log collection
  • Service restarts
  • Targeted healing
  • Single-node or small-scope corrective actions

No YAML DSL. No configuration drift. No multi-stage pipelines. Just run the action.

docs.title: 2. Zero-Agent Architecture
docs.text:
Remote hosts do not run RUNE.

  • No daemon
  • No background agent
  • No bootstrapping
  • No dependency installation

RUNE executes actions using:

  • SSH (default)
  • AWS SSM (optional for cloud nodes)

This keeps your fleet clean and minimizes blast radius. RUNE is not another moving part you have to maintain.

docs.title: 3. Standards-Based Protocol Design
docs.text: RUNE is not a pile of shell scripts glued together. It is built on explicit, documented protocols:

docs.title: Runtime Communication Spec (RCS)
docs.text: Defines how requests and responses are structured and routed.

docs.title: Error Protocol Specification (EPS)
docs.text:
A strict, JSON‑RPC–inspired error envelope with:

  • error codes
  • context
  • recovery hints
  • machine‑parseable structure

docs.title: Bash Plugin Communication Spec (BPCS)
docs.text:
Defines exactly how Bash plugins must:

  • receive input (via JSON stdin)
  • output results (structured JSON stdout)
  • report errors (exit codes + EPS objects)

docs.title: Module Registration Spec (MRS)
docs.text:
Plugins can self‑describe capabilities for introspection.

These standards make RUNE:

  • predictable
  • testable
  • observable
  • extensible

And unlike many tools, none of these protocols are proprietary black boxes.

docs.title: 4. Simple, Replaceable Plugin Model
docs.text:
A RUNE plugin is just a script:

  • Bash today
  • Python tomorrow
  • Any language that can read stdin and print JSON is fair game

There is no DSL to learn and no vendor lock‑in.

Your team already knows how to write RUNE plugins because they already know Bash.

docs.title: 5. Minimal Surface Area, Maximum Reliability
docs.text:
RUNE focuses on one thing: remote execution with structured results.

It is intentionally not a:

  • config‑management framework
  • workflow engine
  • cluster scheduler
  • monitoring pipeline
  • GitOps platform

This minimalism means:

  • faster onboarding
  • fewer failure modes
  • cleaner logs and traces
  • easier security audits

RUNE does not try to be everything — just the thing you need during incident response.

docs.title: 6. Designed for Both Human and Machine Users
docs.text:
RUNE serves two audiences:

  • Humans running rune run gather-logs --node host01
  • Systems (dashboards, alert handlers, CI/CD pipelines) calling RUNE to execute emergency actions

Its output is always structured JSON, so:

  • dashboards can show results cleanly
  • automation can react programmatically
  • logs can be indexed and correlated

docs.title: 7. Plays Nicely With Existing Ecosystems
docs.text:
RUNE is not an Ansible replacement. It is not trying to be Salt. It does not compete with StackStorm.

It complements them:

  • Use Ansible to configure your fleet
  • Use Salt for reactive state enforcement
  • Use StackStorm for workflows
  • Use RUNE for immediate remediation and diagnostics

RUNE fills the missing middle ground.

docs.title: Focused - Only solves the problem it was born to solve
Agentless - Zero footprint on remote nodes
Structured - Everything uses known schemas and standards
Composable - Easy to integrate into dashboards or pipelines
Predictable - No ad‑hoc output, no guessing, no surprises
docs.text:
RUNE is small by design, but powerful in the exact moments you need it.

If Ansible is a Swiss Army knife and Salt is a multi‑tool—RUNE is the scalpel.

docs.title: Comparison Diagram: RUNE vs Other Platforms
docs.text:
graph LR
    A[Ansible] -->|Configuration Management| CM
    S[SaltStack] -->|State Enforcement & Events| CM
    X[StackStorm] -->|Workflows & Automation| CM
    C[Chef/Puppet] -->|Declarative Infra| CM
    R[RUNE] -->|Targeted Remediation & Immediate Actions| RM

    CM["Large Automation Platforms"]
    RM["Focused Remediation Layer"]

    R -.->|Complements| A
    R -.->|Complements| S
    R -.->|Complements| X

    R:::highlight

classDef highlight fill:#005f88,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff;

This diagram highlights RUNE’s role as a remediation‑layer tool, not a config‑management competitor.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This project follows PEP-8 with modern Python best practices and some intentional deviations documented below.

docs.title: Line Length
docs.text:
Maximum: 99 characters

Rationale:

  • Modern standard (black/ruff default)
  • Better for modern displays and IDEs
  • Balances readability with practicality
  • Reduces unnecessary line breaks

Note: This is an intentional deviation from strict PEP-8 (79 characters) but aligns with modern Python community practices and tooling defaults.

docs.title: Type Hints
docs.text: Style: Python 3.10+ syntax with from __future__ import annotations

docs.title: Modern Type Syntax
docs.text:
from __future__ import annotations

# Use built-in generics (not typing.List, typing.Dict, etc.)
def process_items(items: list[str]) -> dict[str, int]:
    """Process a list of items and return counts."""
    ...

# Use union operator for optional types (not typing.Optional)
def get_value(key: str) -> str | None:
    """Return value or None if not found."""
    ...

# Use union operator for unions (not typing.Union)
def parse_data(data: str | bytes) -> dict[str, Any]:
    """Parse string or bytes data."""
    ...

# Use tuple for fixed-length sequences
def get_coordinates() -> tuple[float, float]:
    """Return x, y coordinates."""
    ...

docs.title: Type Hints Best Practices
docs.text:
• Always use type hints on public function parameters and return types
  • Use from __future__ import annotations at the top of modules
  • Prefer built-in types over typing module equivalents
  • Keep typing imports only for special types (Any, Callable, TypedDict, etc.)
  • Annotate complex types to improve code clarity
  • Use type aliases for complex or repeated types
from __future__ import annotations

from pathlib import Path
from typing import Any, Callable

# Type alias for clarity
PathLike = str | Path

def process_file(path: PathLike, callback: Callable[[str], Any]) -> None:
    """Process a file with a callback function."""
    ...

docs.title: Docstrings
docs.text: Format: PEP-257 compliant with Google-style parameter documentation

docs.title: Module Docstrings
docs.text:
"""Short module description.

Longer description providing more context about the module's purpose,
main functionality, and usage patterns.
"""

docs.title: Function Docstrings
docs.text:
def function_name(arg1: str, arg2: int) -> bool:
    """Short description of what the function does.

    Longer description if needed, explaining the function's behavior,
    side effects, or important implementation details.

    Args:
        arg1: Description of first argument
        arg2: Description of second argument

    Returns:
        Description of return value

    Raises:
        ValueError: When arg2 is negative
        FileNotFoundError: When file doesn't exist
    """
    ...

docs.title: Class Docstrings
docs.text:
class ClassName:
    """Short description of the class.

    Longer description explaining the class purpose, main responsibilities,
    and usage patterns.

    Attributes:
        attr1: Description of attribute
        attr2: Description of attribute
    """

    def __init__(self, param: str) -> None:
        """Initialize the class.

        Args:
            param: Description of parameter
        """
        ...

docs.title: Imports
docs.text:
Order:

  1. Standard library imports
  2. Third-party package imports
  3. Local application imports

Grouping: Separate each group with a blank line

Style: One import per line for clarity

# Standard library
import os
import sys
from pathlib import Path

# Third-party
import click
import requests

# Local
from .core import process_file
from .data import load_data

docs.title: Naming Conventions
docs.text: Type Convention Example Functions snake_case def process_data(): Variables snake_case user_name = "John" Constants UPPER_SNAKE_CASE MAX_SIZE = 100 Classes PascalCase class UserProfile: Exceptions PascalCase class ValidationError: Private _leading_underscore def _internal_func(): Protected _leading_underscore self._protected_attr Dunder __double_underscore__ __init__, __str__

docs.title: Module Structure
docs.text:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# SPDX-FileCopyrightText: 2025 Author Name <email@example.com>
# SPDX-License-Identifier: LICENSE-ID

"""Module docstring."""

from __future__ import annotations

# Imports (grouped as above)
import os
from pathlib import Path

# Constants
MAX_RETRIES = 3
DEFAULT_TIMEOUT = 30

# Type aliases
PathLike = str | Path

# Classes
class MyClass:
    ...

# Functions
def my_function():
    ...

# Main execution
if __name__ == "__main__":
    ...

docs.title: Function Length
docs.text:
• Target: <20-30 lines per function
  • Rationale: Easier to understand, test, and maintain
  • Refactor long functions into smaller, focused helper functions

docs.title: Single Responsibility
docs.text:
• Each function should do one thing well
  • Each class should have one reason to change
  • Extract complex logic into separate functions

docs.title: String Formatting
docs.text:
Prefer f-strings:

# Good
name = "Alice"
message = f"Hello, {name}!"

# Avoid
message = "Hello, %s!" % name
message = "Hello, {}!".format(name)

docs.title: Path Handling
docs.text:
Prefer pathlib.Path:

from pathlib import Path

# Good
path = Path("data") / "file.txt"
if path.exists():
    content = path.read_text()

# Avoid
import os
path = os.path.join("data", "file.txt")
if os.path.exists(path):
    with open(path) as f:
        content = f.read()

docs.title: Context Managers
docs.text:
Always use with for resources:

# Good
with open("file.txt") as f:
    content = f.read()

# Avoid
f = open("file.txt")
content = f.read()
f.close()

docs.title: Comprehensions
docs.text:
Use when readable:

# Good - clear and concise
squares = [x**2 for x in range(10)]
evens = [x for x in numbers if x % 2 == 0]

# Avoid - too complex
result = [
    process(x, y, z)
    for x in items
    for y in x.subitems
    for z in y.subsubitems
    if condition(x, y, z)
]

docs.title: Enums
docs.text:
Use for fixed sets:

from enum import Enum

class Status(Enum):
    PENDING = "pending"
    APPROVED = "approved"
    REJECTED = "rejected"

docs.title: EAFP vs LBYL
docs.text:
Prefer EAFP (Easier to Ask Forgiveness than Permission):

# Good - EAFP
try:
    value = config[key]
except KeyError:
    value = default

# Avoid - LBYL
if key in config:
    value = config[key]
else:
    value = default

docs.title: Specific Exceptions
docs.text:
Catch specific exceptions:

# Good
try:
    data = json.loads(text)
except json.JSONDecodeError as exc:
    logger.error(f"Invalid JSON: {exc}")
    raise

# Avoid
try:
    data = json.loads(text)
except Exception:
    pass

docs.title: Exception Context
docs.text:
Preserve exception context:

# Good
try:
    result = risky_operation()
except ValueError as exc:
    msg = f"Operation failed: {exc}"
    raise RuntimeError(msg) from exc

# Avoid
try:
    result = risky_operation()
except ValueError:
    raise RuntimeError("Operation failed")

docs.title: Formatter
docs.text:
black (line-length=99)

# Format all code
black src/ tests/

# Check formatting
black --check src/ tests/

docs.title: Linters
docs.text:
ruff (comprehensive linting)

# Lint code
ruff check src/ tests/

# Auto-fix issues
ruff check --fix src/ tests/

isort (import sorting)

# Sort imports
isort src/ tests/

# Check import order
isort --check-only src/ tests/

docs.title: Type Checker
docs.text:
mypy (static type checking)

# Type check code
mypy src/

# Strict mode
mypy --strict src/

docs.title: Quick Check
docs.text:
# Format, lint, and test
make quick-check

docs.title: Individual Checks
docs.text:
# Format code
make format

# Run linters
make lint

# Run type checker
mypy src/

# Run tests
make test

# Run all checks
make check

Install pre-commit hooks to automatically check code before committing:

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
This style guide ensures:

  • ✅ Consistent, readable code
  • ✅ Modern Python practices
  • ✅ Strong type safety
  • ✅ Maintainable codebase

Key Principles:

  1. Clarity over cleverness
  2. Explicit over implicit
  3. Simple over complex
  4. Tested over untested
  5. Documented over undocumented

docs.title: RUNE System Architecture
docs.text: This document provides a high-level view of RUNE's core components and their interaction during plugin execution. This MVP design favors clarity, modularity, and minimal surface area for deployment.

docs.title: High-Level Architecture (MVP)
docs.text:
flowchart TD
  D[SoC Dashboard or Operator] -->|invoke| C[RUNE CLI]
  C -->|exec request| O[LOM -Orchestrator-]
  O -->|build payload| M[LMM -Mediator-]
  M -->|run remote| T[SSH / SSM Transport]
  T -->|execute| H[Remote Host]
  H -->|invoke| P[Bash Plugin]

  %% Response path (dashed)
  P -.->|JSON stdout + exit| H
  H -.-> T
  T -.-> M
  M -.-> O
  O -.-> C
  C -.->|result / exit code| D

docs.title: CLI
docs.text:
• Primary entrypoint for users and systems
  • Parses commands, invokes LOM

docs.title: LOM – Lifecycle Orchestrator Module
docs.text:
• Validates requested action
  • Selects appropriate plugin
  • Delegates execution to LMM

docs.title: LMM – Local Mediation Module
docs.text:
• Handles all plugin execution and communication
  • Ensures JSON payload validity and formatting
  • Routes structured success/failure messages back to LOM

docs.title: Remote Execution
docs.text:
• Agentless invocation via:

  • SSH with bash -s

  • AWS SSM send-command (optional)

docs.title: Plugins (Remote Scripts)
docs.text:
• Perform the requested task
  • Conform to Bash Plugin Communication Spec (BPCS)
  • Must produce structured JSON and exit codes

docs.title: Message Protocols
docs.text: Purpose Spec Action Request RCS (Runtime Comm) Registration MRS (Module Registry) Error Reporting EPS (Error Protocol)

docs.title: Observability Hooks
docs.text:
• All actions return structured JSON
  • stdout: primary channel for result object
  • stderr: reserved for debug, logging, or trace IDs
  • Optional: logs/telemetry shipped to centralized sink

docs.title: Design Notes
docs.text:
• All modules are decoupled and replaceable
  • No direct plugin-to-plugin interaction
  • Core system is Python-based; plugins can be any language that conforms to the protocol
  • Architecture supports eventual transition to event-driven workflows without overhaul

docs.title: Contributing to RUNE
docs.text: Thank you for your interest in contributing! RUNE is an early-stage but fast-moving project. Here’s how to get started.

docs.title: 🔧 Project Setup
docs.text:
1. Fork the repo and clone it locally:

bash git clone https://github.com/<your-username>/rune.git cd rune

  1. Set up a virtualenv:

bash python3 -m venv .venv source .venv/bin/activate pip install -e .[dev]

  1. Run CLI locally:

bash python rune_cli.py run noop --node testhost

docs.title: ✍️ Contributing a Plugin
docs.text:
1. Create a Bash script in plugins/
  2. Follow the Plugin Development Guide
  3. Test it with example JSON piped into stdin
  4. Make sure it returns structured JSON and exits correctly

docs.title: 🧪 Running Tests
docs.text:
pytest tests/

Add tests for any new modules you create under rune/ or plugins/.

docs.title: 🔁 Pull Request Process
docs.text:
• Fork → Feature branch → PR
  • Reference an issue (or create one)
  • Ensure all tests pass and no linting errors
  • Document any changes in docs/

docs.title: 🙏 Community Standards
docs.text: Be respectful. No drama. Fix bugs. Write useful things.

docs.title: 🔍 Where to Help
docs.text:
• Plugin development
  • CLI enhancements
  • SSM transport fallback
  • Plugin registry/metadata logic
  • Docs, tests, diagrams

Thanks for improving RUNE!

docs.title: RUNE Developer Overview
docs.text:
Welcome to the RUNE Developer Portal. This section of the documentation is intended for anyone who extends RUNE beyond its core capabilities—whether by writing plugins, authoring larger modules, or implementing integrations that communicate with RUNE’s internal protocols.

This guide provides a high‑level map of the developer ecosystem and links to deeper resources.

docs.title: Plugins
docs.text:
Plugins are executable components—most commonly Bash scripts—that RUNE invokes to perform targeted tasks. Plugins:

  • Take structured input via BPCS (Bash Plugin Communication Spec)
  • Perform an action on the host
  • Return structured output back to RUNE

Typical examples:

  • Restarting services
  • Collecting logs or health data
  • Repairing common issues

Plugins may be:

  • Simple (a few lines, using rune_out / rune_err / rune_finish)
  • Advanced (building their own JSON with jq and using rune_ok / rune_error)

docs.title: Modules
docs.text:
Modules bundle multiple plugins and optionally add shared logic, configuration, or state. Modules are appropriate when:

  • A feature requires several related actions
  • Behavior needs reuse across plugins
  • The extension becomes larger than a single script

Modules are the preferred structure for significant RUNE extensions.

docs.title: Protocols
docs.text:
RUNE’s internal communication and plugin interface are defined by several stable protocols:

  • BPCS — Bash Plugin Communication Specification
  • RCS — RUNE Communication Schema
  • Envelope Specification — standardized message format
  • Routing & Observability — metadata and tracing schema

Understanding these protocols is essential for advanced plugin development and any module that communicates deeply with the RUNE orchestrator.

docs.title: Developer Documentation Structure
docs.text: This section of the docs is organized into three major tracks.

docs.title: 1. Plugin Development
docs.text:
Start here if you want to write scripts that RUNE can execute.

  • Beginner plugin tutorial
  • Advanced JSON techniques
  • Full BPCS library API reference

docs.title: 2. Module Development
docs.text:
For larger, multi-plugin projects that encapsulate reusable logic.

  • Module development guide
  • Testing and validation
  • Security model and best practices

docs.title: 3. Protocols
docs.text:
Reference specifications that define how RUNE communicates with plugins and modules.

  • BPCS specification
  • RCS schema
  • Envelope and metadata formats

docs.title: Who This Section Is For
docs.text:
You should use this section if you are:

  • A systems engineer creating automation or repair scripts
  • A platform engineer extending RUNE behavior
  • A developer implementing deeper integrations with the RUNE runtime
  • Anyone who needs to understand how RUNE communicates with external components

If you instead want to install or operate RUNE, visit the Implementers section. If you want to work on RUNE’s internal source code, see Project → Contributing.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: If you are new to RUNE development, begin with:

docs.title: 👉 Plugin Quickstart (Beginner)
docs.text:
A simple introduction to writing plugins using the new accumulator API.

If you already know Bash and want full control over JSON:

docs.title: 👉 Advanced Plugin JSON Guide
docs.text:
Teaches how to construct precise JSON output using jq and the low-level APIs.

For large-scale extensions:

docs.title: 👉 Module Development Guide
docs.text: Explains structuring, packaging, and testing modules.

docs.title: Need Help?
docs.text:
If you want additional examples, architecture diagrams, or deeper explanations, reach out or check the RUNE Implementation Guide for broader conceptual context.

Welcome to the RUNE developer ecosystem—build boldly!

docs.title: RUNE API Reference (Planned Interface)
docs.text: This document outlines the future RUNE REST API for triggering actions, introspecting available plugins, and querying status. It is not required for MVP, but defines the planned structure for dashboard and programmatic integration.

docs.title: Base URL
docs.text:
POST /v1/run
GET  /v1/actions
GET  /v1/status/:job_id

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
• Bearer token or mutual TLS (future)
  • Headers:

http Authorization: Bearer <token> Content-Type: application/json

docs.title: POST /v1/run
docs.text: Trigger a remote action on a node.

docs.title: Request Body
docs.text:
{
  "action": "gather-logs",
  "node": "host-123",
  "parameters": {
    "duration": "5m"
  },
  "trace_id": "optional-guid-here"
}

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "job_id": "abc123",
  "status": "submitted",
  "trace_id": "abc123",
  "message": "Action accepted"
}

docs.title: GET /v1/actions
docs.text: List available actions (via MRS metadata).

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
[
  {
    "name": "restart-docker",
    "description": "Restarts Docker service",
    "input_schema": {...},
    "output_schema": {...}
  }
]

docs.title: GET /v1/status/:job_id
docs.text: Retrieve execution status and results.

docs.title: Response (Success)
docs.text:
{
  "job_id": "abc123",
  "status": "complete",
  "result": {
    "output_data": {
      "archive": "/tmp/logs.tar.gz"
    },
    "exit_code": 0
  }
}

docs.title: Response (Failure)
docs.text:
{
  "job_id": "abc123",
  "status": "failed",
  "error": {
    "code": -32001,
    "message": "Non-zero exit code",
    "data": {
      "plugin": "restart-nomad",
      "exit_code": 1
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Execution is asynchronous
  • job_id and trace_id are UUIDs
  • Internally routes through LOM → LMM → plugin → back to client
  • Response conforms to RCS or EPS

© 2025 Richard Majewski. Licensed under the MPL-2.0.

docs.title: RUNE Security Model
docs.text: This document outlines the security principles and safeguards embedded in RUNE's MVP design. The goal is to ensure remote actions are controlled, auditable, and constrained by least privilege.

docs.title: Threat Model
docs.text:
RUNE is designed to execute remote commands across Linux systems triggered by operators or automated systems. Key risks:

  • Unauthorized action execution
  • Plugin misuse or privilege escalation
  • Leakage of secrets or sensitive output
  • Insecure transport of execution payloads

docs.title: Security Principles
docs.text:
• Agentless by default: No long-running daemons on endpoints
  • Transport abstraction: SSH/SSM handles identity, encryption, and auth
  • Explicit plugin invocation: No dynamic code injection or reflection
  • Structured I/O: Prevents script injection or sloppy command output parsing

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
• SSH: Leverages host/user SSH keys, sudo restrictions, or bastion models
  • SSM: IAM-based authentication tied to instance profile or SSO identity

docs.title: Authorization (MVP Scope)
docs.text:
• CLI can restrict which users can run which plugins
  • Plugin whitelist enforced by LOM
  • Future: RBAC policy engine tied to user/session metadata

docs.title: Plugin Execution Boundaries
docs.text:
• Plugins must run as non-root unless explicitly whitelisted
  • RUNE can enforce sudo -u restricted_user when calling plugins
  • Output parsing is validated before being accepted

docs.title: Secrets Handling
docs.text:
• No secrets should be embedded in plugin code
  • Plugins should reference external secret stores (e.g. Vault, SSM Parameter Store)
  • Future RUNE version may inject secrets securely via environment or temp file

docs.title: Audit & Logging
docs.text:
• All invocations should log:

  • Timestamp

  • Target node
  • Action invoked

  • Exit status / trace ID

  • Logs written locally and/or sent to centralized observability sink

docs.title: Hardening Recommendations
docs.text:
• Restrict plugin directory access to root or ops group
  • Use SSH key constraints (from=, command=)
  • Use session-level trace ID for every action
  • Disable direct shell access to plugin execution host if possible

RUNE is not a privilege escalator—it assumes least privilege at every layer. All actions must be deliberate, accountable, and traceable.

© 2025 Richard Majewski. Licensed under the MPL-2.0.

docs.title: RUNE Testing Guide
docs.text: This guide describes how to validate RUNE core logic, plugin behavior, and overall integration using structured tests.

docs.title: Unit Tests
docs.text:
Located in tests/

  • Test rune_cli.py, orchestrator.py, mediator.py
  • Use mocks for SSH/SSM transport to avoid real remote execution

docs.title: Plugin Tests
docs.text:
• Pipe mock input JSON into each plugin
  • Capture and validate structured JSON output
  • Ensure correct exit codes and error handling

docs.title: Integration Tests
docs.text:
• Simulate end-to-end execution:

bash rune run restart-docker --node testhost --dry-run

  • Verify orchestration, payload formatting, transport call, and output parsing

docs.title: 🧪 Plugin Testing Example
docs.text:
echo '{
  "payload": { "data": { "input_parameters": {} } }
}' | ./plugins/restart-docker.sh | jq .
  • Output must be valid JSON
  • Exit code 0 for success
  • Exit 1+ for failure with proper error key

docs.title: ✅ Test Checklist
docs.text:
• [ ] All core modules have unit test coverage ≥ 80%
  • [ ] Every plugin has a test case and error case
  • [ ] Transport fallbacks (mocked)
  • [ ] CLI flags are respected in tests

docs.title: Tools
docs.text:
• pytest
  • unittest.mock
  • jq (for CLI-level JSON parsing)
  • GitHub Actions (CI-ready)

Stay strict. RUNE actions must always behave predictably, or not at all.

© 2025 Richard Majewski. Licensed under the MPL-2.0.

docs.title: Advanced Admin Guide: Direct JSON Construction with rune_ok / rune_error
docs.text:
This guide is for experienced Bash users who want full control over the JSON returned to RUNE. Instead of using the beginner-friendly accumulator functions (rune_out, rune_err, rune_finish), you will:

  • Write JSON manually using jq -n

  • Pass it directly to rune_ok or rune_error

  • Build nested structures, arrays, complex types, etc.

This method provides the most power and flexibility.

docs.title: 🚀 1. When You Should Use This Method
docs.text:
Use direct JSON construction when:

  • You need nested objects or arrays (not just flat key=value)

  • Fields require type correctness (numbers, booleans, nulls)

  • Your output must follow a specific schema

  • The fix‑it script produces complex diagnostics

  • You need full control of error formatting

If your script is simple, the beginner approach is recommended.

docs.title: ⚠️ 2. Direct JSON Output Pattern
docs.text: You are responsible for building output_data or error.data.

docs.title: Example JSON construction via jq:
docs.text:
OUTPUT=$(jq -n \
  --arg service "$SERVICE" \
  --argjson attempts "$ATTEMPTS" \
  '{service: $service, attempts: $attempts}')

Then pass it to RUNE:

rune_ok "Service restarted" "$OUTPUT"

docs.title: Or on error:
docs.text:
ERRDATA=$(jq -n --arg reason "$REASON" '{reason: $reason}')
rune_error 2001 "Restart failed" "$ERRDATA"

docs.title: 🧠 3. Pros & Cons of the Advanced JSON Method
✅ Pros (Why senior admins prefer it)
docs.text:
• Full control of JSON structure

  • Supports nested objects / arrays

  • Strong typing: numbers, booleans, null

  • Output can match external schemas

  • Most flexible for automation, reporting, observability

docs.title: ❌ Cons
docs.text:
• Requires jq knowledge

  • Slightly more verbose

  • More opportunities for syntax errors

  • Must ensure JSON is valid

docs.title: 📘 4. Direct Access to All Metadata and Parameters
docs.text:
You can use:

rune_param "key" "default"
rune_param_json "key" "{}"
rune_meta "key" "default"

But now you also have the option to embed data directly into JSON.

docs.title: Example:
docs.text:
NODE=$(rune_meta "node" "unknown")
PARAMS=$(rune_all_params)   # raw JSON map

Embed these directly:

OUTPUT=$(jq -n \
  --arg node "$NODE" \
  --argjson params "$PARAMS" \
  '{node: $node, parameters: $params}')

docs.title: 🛠️ 5. Example: Service Restart with Structured Output
docs.text:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
. /opt/rune/lib/rune_bpcs.sh
rune_init

SERVICE=$(rune_param "service" "nginx")
ATTEMPTS=$(rune_param "attempts" "1")

NODE=$(rune_meta "node" "unknown")

attempt_counter=0
success=false

while (( attempt_counter < ATTEMPTS )); do
    if systemctl restart "$SERVICE"; then
        success=true
        break
    fi
    attempt_counter=$((attempt_counter + 1))
done

if [[ "$success" == true ]]; then
    OUTPUT=$(jq -n \
      --arg service "$SERVICE" \
      --argjson attempts "$ATTEMPTS" \
      --arg node "$NODE" \
      '{service: $service, attempts: $attempts, node: $node, restarted: true}')
    rune_ok "Service restarted successfully" "$OUTPUT"
else
    ERRDATA=$(jq -n \
      --arg service "$SERVICE" \
      --argjson attempts "$ATTEMPTS" \
      --arg node "$NODE" \
      '{service: $service, attempts: $attempts, node: $node, restarted: false}')
    rune_error 2100 "Service restart failed" "$ERRDATA"
fi

docs.title: 🧰 6. Example: Returning a Nested JSON Structure
docs.text:
ROOT=$(mktemp -d)
tar -czf "$ROOT/archive.tar.gz" /var/log

OUTPUT=$(jq -n \
  --arg path "$ROOT/archive.tar.gz" \
  --argjson stats '{lines:1204, size_mb:12.4}' \
  '{artifact: {path: $path, stats: $stats}, status: "ok"}')

rune_ok "archive complete" "$OUTPUT"

This creates:

{
  "artifact": {
    "path": "/tmp/archive.tar.gz",
    "stats": {"lines":1204,"size_mb":12.4}
  },
  "status": "ok"
}

docs.title: ✔ Use jq for all JSON creation
docs.text: Never hardcode JSON with echo.

docs.title: ✔ Validate complex JSON during development
docs.text: echo "$OUTPUT" | jq .

docs.title: ✔ Keep output small and meaningful
docs.text: Too much data slows transfer.

docs.title: ✔ Always supply numeric fields using --argjson
docs.text: --argjson count "$COUNT"

docs.title: ✔ Use snake_case keys for consistency
docs.text: Example: artifact_path, disk_usage, restart_attempts.

docs.title: ✔ Prefer structured objects over unstructured strings
docs.text:
Good:

{"stats": {"lines": 120, "errors": 2}}

Not good:

{"stats": "120 lines, 2 errors"}

docs.title: 🧩 8. Combining Advanced and Beginner Methods (Hybrid Mode)
docs.text:
You can mix:

  • rune_out for simple fields

  • manual JSON for complex structures

  • rune_finish to finalize

But if you’re building advanced JSON, it’s cleaner to:

  • Stick entirely to rune_ok / rune_error

  • Build one clean output payload

docs.title: 🎓 9. Summary
docs.text:
This advanced method gives you:

  • Precision

  • Structure

  • Strong typing

  • Flexibility

Use it when creating:

  • Diagnostic tools

  • Report generators

  • Nested or typed data outputs

  • Anything that requires more than simple key/values

If your script is simple, choose the beginner version for speed and clarity.

docs.title: Beginner Admin Guide: Writing Fix‑It Scripts with rune_out, rune_err, and rune_finish
docs.text:
This guide is written for newer or occasional Bash scripters who need to write fix‑it scripts for RUNE. You do not need to know JSON, jq, or advanced Bash. The shared library (rune_bpcs.sh) handles everything for you.

Your job is simply to:

  1. Read inputs using rune_param / rune_meta

  2. Add return values using rune_out

  3. Report failures using rune_err

  4. End the script using rune_finish

That’s it!

docs.title: ✅ 1. Required Script Template
docs.text:
Every fix‑it plugin should start like this:

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

. /opt/rune/lib/rune_bpcs.sh
rune_init

And end like this:

rune_finish "optional success message"

Everything else happens between those lines.

docs.title: ✅ 2. Reading Inputs
docs.text:
Inputs come from RUNE as parameters.

VALUE=$(rune_param "name_of_param" "default_value")

Examples:

SERVICE=$(rune_param "service" "nginx")
FORCE=$(rune_param "force" "false")

Metadata (like which node you’re on):

NODE=$(rune_meta "node" "unknown")

docs.title: ✅ 3. Returning Output Values
docs.text:
Instead of printing text, you send structured data using:

rune_out key value

Examples:

rune_out service "$SERVICE"
rune_out restarted "true"
rune_out log_path "/tmp/logs.tar.gz"

These values become part of the JSON RUNE receives — but you never have to write JSON.

docs.title: ❌ 4. Reporting Errors (Don’t exit manually!)
docs.text:
If something fails:

rune_err 2001 "Failed to restart service"

You can still add additional fields:

rune_out service "$SERVICE"
rune_err 2002 "Permission denied restarting service"

RUNE will treat the script as failed after rune_finish is called.

docs.title: ✅ 5. Finalizing Execution
docs.text:
Your script must call:

rune_finish

This outputs the final JSON response and exits with the right code.

If you want to include a custom success message:

rune_finish "cleanup complete"

docs.title: ⭐ 6. Beginner Example #1: Restart a Service
docs.text:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
. /opt/rune/lib/rune_bpcs.sh
rune_init

SERVICE=$(rune_param "service" "nginx")
NODE=$(rune_meta "node" "unknown")

echo "Restarting $SERVICE on $NODE" >&2

if systemctl restart "$SERVICE"; then
    rune_out service "$SERVICE"
    rune_out restarted "true"
else
    rune_out service "$SERVICE"
    rune_out restarted "false"
    rune_err 2001 "Failed to restart service"
fi

rune_finish "service restart"

docs.title: ⭐ 7. Beginner Example #2: Collect Logs
docs.text:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
. /opt/rune/lib/rune_bpcs.sh
rune_init

WORKDIR=$(mktemp -d)
TARFILE=$(mktemp /tmp/logs-XXXXXX.tar.gz)

cp /var/log/syslog "$WORKDIR"/syslog 2>/dev/null || true
cp /var/log/messages "$WORKDIR"/messages 2>/dev/null || true

tar -czf "$TARFILE" -C "$WORKDIR" .

rune_out artifact_path "$TARFILE"
rune_out message "logs collected"
rune_finish

docs.title: ⚖️ 8. Pros & Cons of the Beginner Method
Pros
docs.text:
• No JSON required — library builds everything

  • Very easy to learn

  • Hard to break

  • Cleaner, shorter scripts

docs.title: Cons
docs.text:
• Less control over JSON structure

  • Output values are all stored as flat key/value pairs

  • Cannot create nested JSON objects

docs.title: 🎯 9. When to Use This Method
docs.text:
Use the beginner API when you want:

  • Fast development

  • Simple fix‑it scripts

  • A low chance of formatting mistakes

  • Clarity and consistency

If you need custom JSON, switch to the mid/senior guide.

docs.title: 🎉 You’re Ready to Write Plugins!
docs.text:
With just rune_out, rune_err, and rune_finish, you can write reliable RUNE fix‑it scripts without ever touching JSON.

If you’d like an even shorter cheat sheet, I can generate one too.

docs.title: Plugin Development Guide for RUNE
docs.text: This guide explains how to create, test, and register Bash- or Python-based plugins for RUNE's MVP framework.

docs.title: Plugin Overview
docs.text:
Plugins are standalone scripts executed on remote systems via SSH or SSM. Each plugin performs a discrete action (e.g., gather logs, restart a service) and must conform to RUNE’s Bash Plugin Communication Spec (BPCS) by reading structured JSON from stdin and returning structured JSON to stdout.

Supported languages:

  • ✅ Bash (primary for MVP)
  • ⬜️ Python (planned)

docs.title: Plugin Structure (Bash Example)
docs.text:
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

# Read stdin into a variable
INPUT=$(cat)

# Extract data (example using jq)
PARAMS=$(echo "$INPUT" | jq -r '.payload.data.input_parameters')
HOSTNAME=$(hostname)

# Main logic (example: archive logs)
LOG_FILE="/tmp/syslog_$HOSTNAME.tar.gz"
tar -czf "$LOG_FILE" /var/log/syslog || {
  echo "{\"error\": \"Log archive failed\"}" && exit 1
}

# Return structured output
cat <<EOF
{
  "message_metadata": {},
  "payload": {
    "result": "success",
    "output_data": {
      "archive": "$LOG_FILE"
    }
  },
  "error": null
}
EOF

docs.title: Required Behavior
docs.text:
• Read structured JSON from stdin
  • Return JSON to stdout with keys: message_metadata, payload, and error
  • Exit 0 on success, non-zero on error
  • Avoid writing logs to stdout—use stderr

docs.title: Plugin Naming
docs.text:
• Use lowercase kebab-case: restart-docker.sh, noop.sh
  • Filename is treated as the plugin identifier
  • File must be executable (chmod +x plugin.sh)

docs.title: Testing Plugins
docs.text:
To test a plugin:

echo '{
  "payload": {
    "data": {
      "input_parameters": {}
    }
  }
}' | ./restart-docker.sh | jq .

Check:

  • Valid JSON output
  • Correct exit code
  • No junk on stdout

docs.title: Registration
docs.text:
LOM/LMM may eventually auto-register plugins (see MRS). For MVP:

  • Plugins are statically mapped in RUNE core
  • Place plugins in plugins/ directory

Future version will support dynamic registration with metadata.

docs.title: Output Example (Success)
docs.text:
{
  "message_metadata": {},
  "payload": {
    "result": "success",
    "output_data": {
      "archive": "/tmp/syslog_web01.tar.gz"
    }
  },
  "error": null
}

docs.title: Output Example (Failure)
docs.text:
{
  "message_metadata": {},
  "payload": null,
  "error": {
    "message": "Log archive failed",
    "code": 1001
  }
}

For plugin execution and message format, see /docs/protocols/protocol_runtime.md (RCS) and /docs/protocols/protocol_error.md (EPS).

© 2025 Richard Majewski. Licensed under the MPL-2.0.

docs.title: RUNE BPCS Library — API Reference
docs.text: This document describes every function provided by rune_bpcs.sh, including inputs, outputs, behavior, and usage notes. This serves as the authoritative API reference for script authors.

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Initialization

  2. rune_init

  3. Parameter Functions

  4. rune_param

  5. rune_param_json

  6. rune_all_params

  7. Metadata Functions

  8. rune_meta

  9. Core Output Functions

  10. rune_ok

  11. rune_error

  12. Accumulator Output System

  13. rune_out

  14. rune_err

  15. rune_finish

  16. Convenience Functions

  17. rune_ok_kv

  18. rune_error_kv

docs.title: Purpose:
docs.text: Reads JSON from stdin, parses core sections, populates internal state for parameter and metadata access.

docs.title: Signature:
docs.text: rune_init

docs.title: Behavior:
docs.text:
• Must be called exactly once, near the top of every plugin.

  • Parses:

  • message_metadata

  • observability

  • routing

  • payload.data.input_parameters

  • Fails if no input is provided.

  • Requires jq to be installed.

docs.title: Returns:
docs.text: Nothing. Initializes global variables.

docs.title: Purpose:
docs.text: Retrieve a string parameter from input_parameters with a fallback default.

docs.title: Signature:
docs.text: value=$(rune_param "key" "default")

docs.title: Inputs:
docs.text:
• key — parameter name.

  • default — value returned if key is missing.

docs.title: Output:
docs.text: A string. Boolean and numeric JSON values are converted to their string equivalents.

docs.title: Purpose:
docs.text: Retrieve a parameter as raw JSON.

docs.title: Signature:
docs.text: json_value=$(rune_param_json "key" "{}")

docs.title: Inputs:
docs.text:
• key — parameter name.

  • default_json — valid JSON value used if missing.

docs.title: Output:
docs.text: A JSON string (compact format).

docs.title: Use case:
docs.text:
• Arrays: extra_paths=["/var/log/syslog", "/tmp/test.log"]

  • Nested objects

docs.title: Purpose:
docs.text: Return the entire input_parameters structure as JSON.

docs.title: Signature:
docs.text: params=$(rune_all_params)

docs.title: Output:
docs.text: A JSON object.

docs.title: Purpose:
docs.text: Retrieve a value from the message_metadata block.

docs.title: Signature:
docs.text: value=$(rune_meta "key" "default")

docs.title: Typical metadata keys:
docs.text:
• node

  • action

  • trace_id

  • request_id

docs.title: Output:
docs.text: String.

docs.title: 📤 4. Core Output Functions
docs.text: These functions print a full JSON response to stdout and exit.

docs.title: Purpose:
docs.text: Send a success response to RUNE.

docs.title: Signature:
docs.text: rune_ok "message" "output_json"

docs.title: Inputs:
docs.text:
• message — short textual summary

  • output_json — JSON object containing results

docs.title: Behavior:
docs.text:
• Prints a JSON structure containing:

  • metadata

  • observability

  • routing

  • payload.result = "success"

  • payload.output_data = provided JSON

  • Exits with code 0.

docs.title: Purpose:
docs.text: Send an error response to RUNE.

docs.title: Signature:
docs.text: rune_error CODE "message" "error_data_json"

docs.title: Inputs:
docs.text:
• CODE — exit code (non‑zero)

  • message — error summary

  • error_data_json — JSON object describing error context

docs.title: Behavior:
docs.text:
• Prints a JSON structure containing:

  • metadata

  • observability

  • routing

  • payload.result = "error"

  • payload.output_data = {…}

  • error.code

  • error.message

  • error.data

  • Exits with code CODE.

docs.title: 📦 5. Accumulator Output System
docs.text: These functions allow script authors to output key/value pairs without building JSON.

docs.title: Purpose:
docs.text: Record a key/value pair to be included in final output.

docs.title: Signature:
docs.text:
rune_out KEY VALUE
# or
rune_out "KEY=VALUE"

docs.title: Inputs:
docs.text:
• KEY VALUE — recommended form

  • "KEY=VALUE" — alternative

docs.title: Behavior:
docs.text: Stores fields internally until rune_finish is called.

docs.title: Output:
docs.text: None.

docs.title: Purpose:
docs.text: Record that an error has occurred.

docs.title: Signature:
docs.text: rune_err CODE "message"

docs.title: Inputs:
docs.text:
• CODE — numeric error code

  • message — description

docs.title: Behavior:
docs.text: Marks script as failed; does not exit immediately.

docs.title: Purpose:
docs.text: Finalize execution based on accumulated output.

docs.title: Signature:
docs.text:
rune_finish
rune_finish "success message"

docs.title: Behavior:
docs.text:
• Builds JSON:

  • If rune_err was called → calls rune_error

  • Otherwise → calls rune_ok

  • Uses all accumulated rune_out fields

  • Exits automatically

docs.title: Output:
docs.text: Structured JSON for RUNE.

docs.title: Purpose:
docs.text: Send a success result using simple key=value pairs.

docs.title: Signature:
docs.text:
rune_ok_kv "message" \
  "key1=value1" \
  "key2=value2"

docs.title: Behavior:
docs.text:
• Converts pairs into a JSON object

  • Calls rune_ok

docs.title: Purpose:
docs.text: Send an error result using simple key=value pairs.

docs.title: Signature:
docs.text:
rune_error_kv CODE "message" \
  "key1=value1" \
  "key2=value2"

docs.title: Behavior:
docs.text:
• Converts pairs into error.data

  • Calls rune_error

docs.title: Installation Guide
docs.text: This document explains how to install, configure, and validate RUNE on a control node. RUNE is agentless—remote hosts require no installation.

docs.title: Control Node
docs.text:
• Linux system (recommended)
  • Python 3.10+
  • Ability to reach remote hosts via SSH or AWS SSM
  • pip or another Python package manager

docs.title: Remote Hosts
docs.text:
• Linux
  • /bin/bash
  • SSH access or SSM connectivity
  • Standard system utilities (depending on plugins)

No Python, no RUNE package, no agents required on remote machines.

docs.title: 2. Install the RUNE CLI
docs.text:
pip install rune-cli

Check installation:

rune --version

If this fails, verify that your PATH includes your Python bin directory.

docs.title: 3. Verify Python Environment
docs.text:
RUNE supports Python 3.10 and newer.

Check your version:

python3 --version

If you need to pin a specific interpreter, create a virtual environment:

python3 -m venv ~/.venv/rune
source ~/.venv/rune/bin/activate
pip install rune-cli

docs.title: 4. Initial Configuration
docs.text:
RUNE uses XDG-style configuration paths.

Create your config directory:

mkdir -p ~/.config/rune

Add a configuration file:

nano ~/.config/rune/config.toml

Minimal SSH-based example:

[core]
default_transport = "ssh"
default_user = "admin"

[ssh]
private_key = "/home/admin/.ssh/id_ed25519"
known_hosts = "/home/admin/.ssh/known_hosts"

Optional SSM configuration:

[ssm]
region = "us-east-1"
iam_role = "arn:aws:iam::<account-id>:role/YourSSMRole"

docs.title: 5. Test Connectivity
docs.text:
Ensure SSH access works:

ssh admin@web01

If using SSM, test permission boundaries via AWS CLI:

aws ssm describe-instance-information

docs.title: 6. Validate RUNE Operation
docs.text:
Test a built-in plugin:

rune run noop --node web01 --output pretty

You should receive structured JSON indicating success.

For remote failures, RUNE returns strict EPS-formatted errors so you can debug quickly.

docs.title: 7. Directory Structure Overview
docs.text:
After installation, RUNE lives in your Python environment:

rune/
  cli.py
  core/
  transports/
  plugins/builtin/
  models/
  config/

User-level plugins can be stored here:

~/.local/share/rune/plugins/

System-wide plugins here:

/opt/rune/plugins/

docs.title: 8. Upgrading RUNE
docs.text:
pip install --upgrade rune-cli

RUNE maintains compatibility across patch versions for all public protocols (RCS, EPS, BPCS).

docs.title: 9. Next Steps
docs.text:
• Quick Start
  • Plugin Development Guide
  • RUNE Implementation Guide
  • System Architecture

RUNE is built to be small, predictable, and safe—everything you need for fast operational response.

© 2025 Richard Majewski. Licensed under the MPL-2.0.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
This guide walks you through using RUNE within minutes. RUNE requires:

  • A Linux control node with Python 3.10+
  • SSH access to your remote hosts (SSM optional)
  • No agents or additional software on remote nodes

docs.title: 1. Install RUNE
docs.text:
pip install rune-cli

Verify installation:

rune --help

docs.title: 2. Configure SSH Access
docs.text:
RUNE defaults to SSH. Ensure you can connect:

ssh admin@web01

Create a config file:

mkdir -p ~/.config/rune
nano ~/.config/rune/config.toml

Example:

[core]
default_transport = "ssh"
default_user = "admin"

[ssh]
private_key = "/home/admin/.ssh/id_ed25519"
known_hosts = "/home/admin/.ssh/known_hosts"

docs.title: 3. Test with the Built‑In noop Plugin
docs.text:
rune run noop --node web01 --output pretty

This validates:

  • SSH connectivity
  • Plugin shipping & execution
  • Mediator parsing & structured output

Expected output:

{
  "payload": {
    "result": "success",
    "output_data": {
      "message": "noop completed"
    }
  },
  "error": null
}

docs.title: 4. Run Real Actions
docs.text:
Gather logs:

rune run gather-logs --node web01

Restart Docker:

rune run restart-docker --node web02

Restart Nomad services:

rune run restart-nomad --node job-runner-1

All actions:

  • Ship a plugin to the remote node
  • Execute it
  • Capture structured JSON output
  • Clean up (unless configured otherwise)

docs.title: 5. Optional: Use AWS SSM
docs.text:
Enable SSM transport in your config:

[core]
default_transport = "ssm"

[ssm]
region = "us-east-1"
iam_role = "arn:aws:iam::<account-id>:role/YourSSMRole"

Execute via SSM:

rune run gather-logs --node i-0123456789abcdef --use-ssm

docs.title: 6. Write Your First Plugin
docs.text:
Example plugin:

#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail
INPUT=$(cat)
cat <<EOF
{
  "payload": {
    "result": "success",
    "output_data": {
      "time": "$(date)"
    }
  },
  "error": null
}
EOF

Save to:

~/.local/share/rune/plugins/

Run:

rune run myplugin --node web01

docs.title: 7. Next Steps
docs.text:
• Why RUNE?
  • System Architecture
  • Plugin Development Guide
  • RUNE Implementation Guide

RUNE is easy to start, powerful to extend.

© 2025 Richard Majewski. Licensed under the MPL-2.0.

docs.title: RUNE Implementation & Deployment Guide
docs.text:
This document explains how RUNE actually runs in the real world:

  • Where the RUNE core lives
  • How it’s installed from PyPI
  • How plugins are stored, shipped, and executed on remote hosts
  • What a typical control-node + fleet setup looks like

This is the “how do I wire this up so it does real work” guide.

docs.title: 1.1 Control Node vs Remote Nodes
docs.text:
RUNE uses a control-node model:

  • RUNE Core (Python) Installed on a single control node (or a small HA set):

  • SoC tooling server

  • Jump/bastion host

  • Admin workstation

  • Remote Nodes (Linux endpoints)

  • No RUNE Python package installed

  • Only requirements:

    • SSH access (or SSM for cloud nodes)
    • /bin/bash (for MVP Bash plugins)
    • Standard system tools (tar, journalctl, systemctl, etc. depending on plugins)

RUNE sends plugins to remote nodes and executes them there; all orchestration, mediation, and logging stay on the control node.

docs.title: 2.1 Basic Install
docs.text:
On the control node:

pip install rune-cli

This provides:

  • A Python package: rune
  • A console script: rune (installed into your venv or system bin)

Check it’s available:

rune --help

docs.title: 2.2 Package Layout (Control Node)
docs.text:
After install, you can expect something like:

rune/
  __init__.py
  cli.py              # CLI entrypoint
  core/
    orchestrator.py   # RUNE Orchestrator (LOM)
    mediator.py       # RUNE Mediator (LMM)
  transports/
    ssh.py            # SSH-based execution
    ssm.py            # Optional AWS SSM-based execution
  plugins/
    builtin/
      gather_logs.sh
      restart_docker.sh
      restart_nomad.sh
      noop.sh
  models/
    rcs.py            # Runtime Communication Spec models
    eps.py            # Error Protocol models
    mrs.py            # Module Registration models
  config/
    defaults.py

You may adjust exact paths, but the idea stands: control-node only, with a clearly defined transport layer and plugin bundle.

docs.title: 3. Runtime Directories & Config
docs.text: RUNE uses standard XDG-style locations (MVP can be simplified if you want).

docs.title: 3.1 Config
docs.text:
Default locations (in order):

  1. ./rune.toml (project-local)
  2. ~/.config/rune/config.toml
  3. /etc/rune/config.toml (system-wide)

Example minimal config.toml:

[core]
default_transport = "ssh"
default_user = "admin"

[ssh]
private_key = "/home/admin/.ssh/id_ed25519"
known_hosts = "/home/admin/.ssh/known_hosts"

[plugins]
# Local paths to plugin scripts on the control node
plugin_dir = "/opt/rune/plugins"

docs.title: 3.2 Local Plugin Storage
docs.text:
RUNE looks for plugins in:

  1. plugins/builtin/ inside the package (shipped with RUNE)
  2. ~/.local/share/rune/plugins/
  3. /opt/rune/plugins/ (or whatever plugin_dir is set to)

You can drop custom scripts in any of those locations and wire them in via config/registry.

docs.title: 4. How Remote Plugins Are Actually Run
docs.text:
Here’s the core of the system: how an action like:

rune run gather-logs --node web01

turns into code running on web01.

docs.title: 4.1 High-Level Flow
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant C as RUNE CLI
    participant O as Orchestrator (LOM)
    participant M as Mediator (LMM)
    participant T as Transport (SSH/SSM)
    participant H as Remote Host
    participant P as Plugin Script

    U->>C: rune run gather-logs --node web01
    C->>O: Parsed command (action=gather-logs, node=web01)
    O->>M: Execution request (RCS payload)
    M->>T: Execute plugin on web01
    T->>H: Copy plugin + run via bash -s
    H->>P: Plugin receives JSON via stdin
    P-->>H: JSON result to stdout, exit code
    H-->>T: stdout/stderr/exit code
    T-->>M: Execution result
    M-->>O: Parsed result or EPS error
    O-->>C: Final structured response
    C-->>U: Human-readable + JSON output

docs.title: 4.2 Plugin Shipping Strategy (MVP)
docs.text:
MVP behavior (simplest, least assumptions):

  1. On the control node, RUNE loads the plugin script from its local plugins/ directory.

  2. RUNE opens a remote session via the configured transport:

  3. SSH: ssh user@web01

  4. SSM: aws ssm send-command ... (future)

  5. RUNE copies the plugin to the remote host, typically to:

  6. /tmp/rune/plugins/gather-logs.sh

  7. RUNE sets perms:

bash chmod +x /tmp/rune/plugins/gather-logs.sh

  1. RUNE runs the plugin remotely with JSON piped to stdin:

bash echo '<JSON-RCS-PAYLOAD>' | /tmp/rune/plugins/gather-logs.sh

  1. The plugin:

  2. Reads JSON from stdin

  3. Performs its task
  4. Writes structured JSON to stdout

  5. Exits with appropriate code

  6. RUNE captures stdout, stderr, and exit code; the Mediator validates and normalizes them.

  7. (Optional) RUNE cleans up:

  8. Remove /tmp/rune/plugins/gather-logs.sh

  9. Or keep a cache per-node for repeat runs

This default “ship and run” mode means you do not need to pre-install anything on remote hosts beyond bash and SSH/SSM.

docs.title: 5. Pre-Installed Plugin Mode (Future/Optional)
docs.text:
In more controlled environments, you may want plugins pre-installed on all hosts (via Ansible, Salt, etc.).

In that mode:

  • RUNE does not copy scripts to remote nodes.

  • Instead, it executes a known path:

bash echo '<JSON>' | /usr/local/lib/rune/plugins/gather-logs.sh

  • The plugin location is configurable:

toml [remote_plugins] base_dir = "/usr/local/lib/rune/plugins"

  • RUNE just ensures the plugin name maps to a file path on the remote system.

MVP can support this with a simple config switch:

[execution]
deploy_mode = "ship"    # or "preinstalled"

docs.title: 6. RUNE Core: Where It Lives and What It Does
6.1 Core is Centralized
docs.text:
The RUNE core:

  • Lives on the control node
  • Is installed as a Python package

  • Owns:

  • CLI interface (rune)

  • Orchestrator (LOM)
  • Mediator (LMM)
  • Transport modules
  • Protocol models (RCS, EPS, MRS)
  • Logging and observability hooks

Remote nodes are dumb execution environments.

docs.title: 6.2 Orchestrator (LOM) Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Validate action name and target
  • Resolve which plugin to use
  • Build standardized RCS payload:

json { "message_metadata": {...}, "routing": {...}, "payload": { "schema_version": "rcs_v1", "data": { "input_parameters": {...} } } }

  • Hand off to Mediator for execution

docs.title: 6.3 Mediator (LMM) Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Call the appropriate transport (SSH or SSM)
  • Ship plugin (if deploy_mode = ship)
  • Execute plugin remotely

  • Capture:

  • stdout (expected JSON)

  • stderr (debug/log)

  • exit code

  • Parse JSON and:

  • On success → return normalized result

  • On failure → wrap in EPS error object

docs.title: 7.1 Control Node Setup
docs.text:
# Install RUNE
pip install rune-cli

# Optional: create config
mkdir -p ~/.config/rune
cat > ~/.config/rune/config.toml <<EOF
[core]
default_transport = "ssh"
default_user = "admin"

[ssh]
private_key = "/home/admin/.ssh/id_ed25519"

[plugins]
plugin_dir = "/opt/rune/plugins"
EOF

docs.title: 7.2 Add a Custom Plugin (Control Node)
docs.text:
sudo mkdir -p /opt/rune/plugins
sudo tee /opt/rune/plugins/check-disk.sh >/dev/null <<'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail
INPUT=$(cat)
# Simple example ignoring input
USE=$(df -h / | awk 'NR==2 {print $5}')
cat <<JSON
{
  "message_metadata": {},
  "payload": {
    "result": "success",
    "output_data": {
      "disk_usage": "$USE"
    }
  },
  "error": null
}
JSON
EOF

sudo chmod +x /opt/rune/plugins/check-disk.sh

Update config mapping if you introduce explicit registry later; for MVP, RUNE can infer from filename → action.

docs.title: 7.3 Running an Action
docs.text:
rune run gather-logs --node web01 --output pretty
rune run restart-docker --node web02
rune run check-disk --node db01 --output json

docs.title: 8. Files & Directories Summary
docs.text: Location Purpose rune/ (Python package) Core orchestrator/mediator logic rune/plugins/builtin/ Built-in plugins shipped with RUNE ~/.config/rune/config.toml User-level config /etc/rune/config.toml System-level config /opt/rune/plugins/ Custom/local plugins /tmp/rune/plugins/ (remote) Shipped plugin scripts on targets site/ (local) Built MkDocs site gh-pages branch Deployed docs for GitHub Pages

docs.title: 9. What Changes When Someone pip install rune-cli?
docs.text:
Once a developer or operator installs the rune-cli package on a control node:

  1. They get the rune CLI.
  2. They get a default set of built-in plugins and protocols.

  3. They only need:

  4. SSH/SSM access to their Linux fleet

  5. Valid config

  6. They can immediately run:

bash rune run noop --node some-host

and start wiring RUNE into:

  • SoC dashboards
  • CI/CD pipelines
  • Runbooks

No agents. No daemons. RUNE core lives in one place and reaches out to do the dirty work.

docs.title: RUNE Protocols Overview
docs.text:
The RUNE Protocols define how information moves between the RUNE Orchestrator, plugins, modules, and other internal or external components. These documents form the contract between RUNE and anything that extends it.

These specifications are technical and normative. They do not describe how to write plugins or modules, but rather how data flows, how messages are shaped, and how RUNE expects components to behave.

docs.title: What Is a Protocol in RUNE?
docs.text:
A RUNE protocol describes:

  • Message shape (required fields, optional fields, data types)
  • Routing behavior (where things go, how they get there)
  • Metadata conventions (trace IDs, actions, observability fields)
  • Execution contract (what a plugin must accept and must return)
  • Schema versioning strategy

These protocols allow:

  • Safe, automated execution of plugins
  • Consistent behavior across all modules
  • Traceable, auditable, and observable workflows
  • Future-proof compatibility

docs.title: 1. BPCS — Bash Plugin Communication Specification
docs.text:
Defines how Bash plugins communicate with RUNE:

  • Input parsing
  • Structured output expectations
  • Error-handling semantics
  • Schema for input parameters, metadata, routing, observability

Used by all Bash-based plugins via rune_bpcs.sh.

docs.title: 2. RCS — RUNE Communication Schema
docs.text:
Defines the envelope and structure RUNE uses internally:

  • Parent message structure
  • Embedding of metadata, routing, and payload
  • Cross-component communication rules

This is the foundational schema for every message RUNE processes.

docs.title: 3. Message Envelope Specification
docs.text:
More detailed description of the message wrapper:

  • Required fields
  • Optional metadata
  • Observability block
  • Forwarding, replication, and transformation rules

docs.title: 4. Routing & Observability Schema
docs.text:
Defines fields related to:

  • Tracing
  • Action lineage
  • Execution timing
  • Route mapping (where a message should go)

This schema is consumed by all high-level RUNE components.

docs.title: Who Should Read These Protocols?
docs.text:
These specs are essential reading for:

  • Plugin developers who want deeper control
  • Module authors implementing complex extensions
  • RUNE contributors modifying internal message handling
  • Anyone implementing a non-Bash plugin system (Python, Go, Rust, etc.)

If you're writing your first plugin, start with:

  • Developers → Plugin Development → Plugin Quickstart (Beginner)

If you're extending RUNE's internals, see:

  • Project → Contributing → Architecture Overview

docs.title: Where to Go Next
docs.text:
Choose the protocol you want to dive into:

  • BPCS Protocol — How Bash plugins interact with RUNE
  • RCS Message Spec — Unified schema for RUNE messages
  • Message Envelope Format — Outer message structure
  • Routing & Observability Schema — Metadata for tracing and routing

Each document provides examples, field definitions, and normative requirements.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: BPCS defines how Bash plugins receive data from RUNE and return structured results. It provides a simple API through a shared Bash library so that plugin authors do not need to manage JSON formatting, parsing, or protocol details.

docs.title: Plugin Data Flow
docs.text:
1. RUNE builds an RCS message.
  2. RUNE sends JSON to the plugin's stdin.
  3. Plugin reads parameters through the shared library.
  4. Plugin executes logic.
  5. Plugin returns JSON through the shared library.
  6. Plugin exits with a meaningful exit code.

docs.title: Shared Library
docs.text:
The shared library (rune_bpcs.sh) handles:

  • Reading and parsing stdin JSON.
  • Retrieving input parameters.
  • Retrieving metadata such as node or trace id.
  • Emitting success or error JSON messages.
  • Managing structured exit codes.

Plugins source the library:

. /opt/rune/lib/rune_bpcs.sh
rune_init

docs.title: Input Structure (stdin)
docs.text:
Plugins receive a trimmed version of RCS:

{
  "message_metadata": {
    "trace_id": "e3a2b5...",
    "action": "restart-docker",
    "node": "web01"
  },
  "payload": {
    "schema_version": "rcs_v1",
    "data": {
      "input_parameters": {
        "force": true,
        "timeout": 30
      }
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Output Structure (stdout)
docs.text: Plugins must emit exactly one JSON object.

docs.title: Success
docs.text:
{
  "payload": {
    "result": "success",
    "output_data": {
      "message": "Docker restarted",
      "service": "docker"
    }
  },
  "error": null
}

docs.title: Error
docs.text:
{
  "payload": null,
  "error": {
    "code": 1001,
    "message": "Failed to restart Docker",
    "data": {
      "exit_code": 1
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Exit Codes
docs.text:
• 0 indicates success.
  • Non zero indicates failure.
  • Plugins should use the shared library's rune_error function.

docs.title: rune_init
docs.text: Reads stdin, parses JSON, initializes library state.

docs.title: rune_param KEY DEFAULT
docs.text: Retrieves an input parameter.

docs.title: rune_meta KEY DEFAULT
docs.text: Retrieves metadata such as action or node.

docs.title: rune_ok MESSAGE JSON_DATA
docs.text: Writes success JSON and exits 0.

docs.title: rune_error CODE MESSAGE JSON_DATA
docs.text: Writes structured error JSON and exits CODE.

docs.title: Example Plugin
docs.text:
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail
. /opt/rune/lib/rune_bpcs.sh
rune_init

SERVICE=$(rune_param "service" "docker")

if systemctl restart "$SERVICE"; then
  rune_ok "Service restarted" '{"service": "'"$SERVICE"'"}'
else
  rune_error 2001 "Restart failed" '{"service": "'"$SERVICE"'"}'
fi

docs.title: Rules
docs.text:
• stdout is reserved for the final JSON output only.
  • stderr may contain diagnostic logs.
  • If a plugin fails without valid JSON, the Mediator will wrap it in an EPS message.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: EPS defines how errors are reported, formatted, routed, and remediated within the RUNE framework. This protocol ensures that all failure modes—whether from remote plugins, internal modules, or transport failures—return a predictable, machine-parseable structure.

docs.title: Scope
docs.text: EPS messages are generated by any RUNE component encountering an error (plugin, LMM, transport, etc.) and routed through the Mediator for centralized handling and logging.

docs.title: Error Message Structure
docs.text:
{
  "routing": {
    "event_type": "EPS_ERROR_RESPONSE",
    "source_module": "mediator",
    "target_module": "error_handler"
  },
  "error_payload": {
    "error_data": {
      "code": -32001,
      "message": "Plugin execution failed",
      "data": {
        "plugin": "restart-docker",
        "host": "web-01",
        "reason": "Non-zero exit code",
        "exit_code": 1
      }
    },
    "error_context": {
      "severity": "medium",
      "recoverable": true,
      "recovery_actions": ["retry", "alert-operator"]
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Field Descriptions
docs.text:
• event_type: Always set to EPS_ERROR_RESPONSE
  • source_module: Origin of the error (e.g., mediator, transport, plugin)
  • target_module: Optional; default is centralized error handler
  • error_data.code: Integer code (negative)
  • error_data.message: Human-readable summary
  • error_data.data: Arbitrary structured context
  • error_context.severity: low, medium, high, or critical
  • error_context.recoverable: Boolean
  • error_context.recovery_actions: Suggested actions (retry, fallback, alert, etc.)

docs.title: Error Generation Rules
docs.text:
• Any plugin that exits non-zero must emit a structured EPS error (if it can)
  • The mediator will wrap stdout/stderr failures in EPS automatically
  • All transport or execution errors must be normalized to EPS

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• EPS is for machine handling, not end-user output (LMM/LOM can format it)
  • Log output from errors should be associated via message_id or trace_id
  • EPS is routed like RCS messages, but always interpreted as an exception path

© 2025 Richard Majewski. Licensed under the MPL-2.0.

docs.title: Module Registration Specification (MRS)
docs.text: Status: Defined and reserved for post-MVP use. The MVP does not yet perform dynamic module registration, but all future capability discovery (e.g., S3 log sinks, Vault integration, external remediation providers) will conform to this specification.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Defines the registration format and process for RUNE modules and plugins. Modules declare their capabilities, supported actions, metadata, and schema versions through this protocol. This specification ensures that the RUNE Orchestrator (LOM) can discover and validate the available actions at runtime.

docs.title: Scope
docs.text: This protocol is invoked during startup, registration time, or when explicitly requested by the LOM. All modules and plugins must comply with this schema to be discoverable.

docs.title: Registration Payload
docs.text:
{
  "registration_metadata": {
    "module_name": "restart-nomad",
    "module_version": "1.0.0",
    "registered_at": "2025-12-01T00:00:00Z"
  },
  "capabilities": [
    {
      "action": "restart-nomad",
      "description": "Restarts Nomad systemd unit",
      "input_schema": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {},
        "required": []
      },
      "output_schema": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
          "result": { "type": "string" }
        },
        "required": ["result"]
      }
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Key Fields
docs.text:
• module_name: Unique, lowercase identifier of the plugin or module
  • module_version: Semantic version of the module or plugin
  • registered_at: RFC 3339 timestamp (UTC)

  • capabilities[]: List of actions this module supports

  • input_schema: JSON Schema object describing expected inputs

  • output_schema: JSON Schema object describing the structure of valid output

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Each module must register exactly once per runtime session
  • Duplicate names will be rejected
  • Version resolution will favor latest for conflicting module names

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• MRS is invoked by LOM directly, not over the runtime channel
  • Capability schemas may be stored for CLI introspection or dashboard use

© 2025 Richard Majewski. Licensed under the MPL-2.0.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Runtime Communication Specification (RCS) defines the JSON message structure used for all operational messaging within RUNE during plugin and module execution. This protocol enables consistent, observable, and traceable communication between the RUNE core (LMM/LOM) and remote plugin runners.

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Plugin execution requests and responses
  • Status reporting from remote scripts
  • Log or data result transmission

docs.title: Message Structure
docs.text:
{
  "message_metadata": {
    "version": "1.0",
    "message_id": "<uuid>",
    "created_at": "<rfc3339 UTC timestamp>",
    "correlation_id": "<uuid, optional>"
  },
  "routing": {
    "event_type": "<action_name>",
    "source_module": "<module or CLI identifier>",
    "reply_to": {
      "event_type": "<expected_response_event>",
      "timeout_ms": 3000
    }
  },
  "payload": {
    "schema_version": "rcs_v1",
    "content_type": "application/json",
    "data": {
      "input_parameters": { ... }
    }
  },
  "observability": {
    "trace_id": "<optional-trace-id>"
  }
}

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• All timestamps must be in RFC 3339 UTC format
  • All message_id and correlation_id values must be UUID v4
  • Payload must contain a valid input_parameters object for plugins

docs.title: Minimal Valid Message
docs.text:
{
  "message_metadata": {
    "version": "1.0",
    "message_id": "...",
    "created_at": "..."
  },
  "routing": {
    "event_type": "gather-logs",
    "source_module": "cli"
  },
  "payload": {
    "schema_version": "rcs_v1",
    "content_type": "application/json",
    "data": {
      "input_parameters": {}
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• RCS does not define how plugins are discovered or registered—that is handled via MRS.
  • Error conditions must be reported using EPS format, not RCS.
  • RCS should remain lightweight to support SSH/SSM transmission.
